summary: postinstall fetches unpinned plugin over plain HTTP from internal registry
details: On `npm install`, postinstall.js probes https://taro.jd.com/ and, if reachable, executes `./bin/taro global-config add-plugin @jdtaro/plugin-build-report-performance@latest --registry http://registry.m.jd.com`. This installs an unpinned (`@latest`) package over plain HTTP from a non-public registry (registry.m.jd.com) and persists the plugin into the user's global Taro config so it auto-loads on every future `taro build`. The behavior is gated on JD-network reachability, so external reviewers and most public installers silently skip the path while installers inside the JD corporate/CI network execute it. The combination — conditional execution keyed on a network probe, plain-HTTP fetch (trivially MITM-able), unpinned mutable `@latest` reference, attacker-controllable registry, and persistence via global plugin auto-load — places arbitrary attacker-controllable code into every subsequent build for any installer that happens to be on the gated network. Even if author intent is internal telemetry, the structure is indistinguishable from a targeted install-time RCE primitive and creates real installer harm for affected users.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
